title: practical english usage - Part 326
content:
After I hope, we  often use a present tense with a future meaning.

1  tenses     after    hope 490 hope I lived there for six years, but I never really felt it was my home.

There are some horrible new houses in our village.

and can express the idea of emotional attachment to a place. Compare: building. Home is used more personally: it is the place that somebody lives in, House is an emotionally neutral word: it just refers to a particular type of 2 house       and   home vocabulary •  492  how How often do you come to New York?

How old are your parents?  How far is your house?

How much  do you weigh?  How many people were there?

These are used to ask for measurements, quantities, etc. Examples: Many interrogative expressions of two or more words begin with how.

5 how     much,      how     many,      how     old,    how     far,    etc How you've changed!

What a wonderful house! How wonderful!

before adjectives (without nouns), adverbs and verb phrases.

ɪn exclamations (►  223), what is used before noun phrases; and how is used What/How about the kids? Who's going to look after them?

What/How about eating out this evening?

and to bring up points that have been forgotten.

Both What about...? and How about...? are used to make suggestions, Why should I think that?

What? What did you say? (     How?-Hew-did-yeu-say?) not What do you think? (     How-do-yow think?) not What's that called? (     How is that called?) not    ow What do you call this? (     H   do you call this?) not How do you know? (     Whydo-you know?) the following common structures.

These three question words can sometimes be confused. Note particularly 4 how,     what      and   why niii (    .. .-how-Mummy  does.) Hold it in both hands, as / like / the way Mummy  does.

In comparisons we  use as or like (►  515) or the way (see below), not how.

3 comparisons:        how     not   used not Show me how fast you can run. (     ... how much  fast-...) not    ow How tall are you? (     H   much  tall are you?) We use how, not how much,  before adjectives and adverbs.

2 with    adjectives/adverbs:            how,     not   how     much not How well she plays! (     How she plays well!) not How beautiful the trees are! (     How the trees are beautiful!) immediately after how.

When  how is used in an exclamation with an adjective or adverb, this comes not    ow How you've grown! (     H   have you-grown!) - How have you been?

How I love weekends! (     Hew de4love weekends!) - How do you like my hair?

as in questions. Compare: We   also use how in exclamations (►  223). The  word order is not the same Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary' •  493   how and what... like?

I was sick three times in the night.

Be sick can meant ‘ vomit’  (= bring food up from the stomach) in British English.

I'm looking after my sick mother. (More normal than ... my ill mother.) Ill is not very common before a noun.

George didn 't come in last week because he was ill/sick.

except in a formal style.) III and sick are both used to mean 'unwell'. (In American English ill is less usual 494 ill   and   sick ►    623 if  and   when ►    371 however,        although,         though       and   but How's your steak? How's the new job?

‘ How was the film? * Very good.' (   What was the film like...?) What.. . like is also possible.

We   often use how to ask about people’ s reactions to their experiences.

2 reactions:      How    was     the    film?

‘ What does she look like? ‘ Short and dark, pretty, cheerful-looking.’ - ‘ How does she look today? ‘ Tired.' ‘ What's Ron like? ‘ He's quiet and a bit shy.’ - ‘ How's Ron? ‘ He's very well.' change - for example people's character and appearance. Compare: moods and health. We   prefer what... like to ask about things that do not We   generally use how to ask about things that change - for example people's 1  changes:     How's    Ron?

493 how     and   what     ...  like?

money is your business), ►    266.

For how-clauses as objects, subjects, etc (e.g. Don’t ask me how the journey was; How you divide up the For however, ►    252 (conjunction), ►    37 1  (adverb).
